primary_reason: The study combines maximum-likelihood phylogenetics, Bayesian phylodynamics, and phylogeography to reconstruct the origins, demographic history, and urban-to-rural spread of HIV-1 in Nigeria.
The study applies phylogenetic, molecular-clock, skyline, selection-pressure, and glycosylation analyses to compare the evolutionary dynamics of influenza B lineages in Vietnam.
The study benchmarks twelve viral haplotype callers against populations simulated under realistic coalescent and evolutionary conditions, identifying diversity-dependent performance limitations.
This review substantively explains how HIV phylogenetics and phylodynamics can reconstruct geographic spread, transmission networks, expanding lineages, and intervention priorities.
The study combines phylogenetic, haplotype-network, and phylodynamic analyses to distinguish local, international, and cross-host dissemination of porcine circovirus in Italy.
This review explains advances in data-linked mechanistic models, uncertainty characterization, forecasting, and phylodynamic inference for infectious-disease dynamics.
The study integrates whole-genome phylogenetics with temporal, geographic, and host metadata to reconstruct persistent and newly introduced Brucella abortus lineages in Costa Rica.
The study uses genetic-distance networks, global phylogenetics, and epidemiologic metadata to characterize introductions and risk-group transmission of HIV-1 subtype B in Bulgaria.
The study uses Bayesian phylodynamics and multi-host viral genomes to infer the timing, source, onward transmission, and evolutionary consequences of canine distemper spillover into lions.
The study applies maximum-likelihood and Bayesian phylogenetics to identify within-group and bridging HIV transmission patterns among four risk populations in coastal Kenya.
The study uses transmission clustering and a birth-death skyline comparison between two cities to evaluate the population-level effect of an HIV prevention intervention.
The paper develops and evaluates a minimum-description-length procedure for selecting the number of change points in epidemic renewal and phylogenetic skyline models.
The study combines whole-genome Bayesian phylogeny, phylogeography, host-trait analysis, and reassortment assessment to reconstruct H9N2 influenza introduction and zoonotic evolution in Tunisia.
The study uses structured coalescent models and environmental covariates to evaluate organizational compartmentalization and drivers of infectious bronchitis virus spread among poultry farms.
The paper develops a unified mechanistic framework using differential and integral equations, networks, probability, and agent-based simulation to compare viral transmission lifestyles and fitness.
The study tests the robustness of common phylodynamic estimates across models and priors and proposes an analytical pipeline intended to reduce biased animal-surveillance inferences.
The study combines phylogenetics, phylodynamics, codon-usage indices, and host-adaptation measures to characterize the evolution of sugarcane mosaic virus.
The study recovers an archival HIV genome and uses root-to-tip, dated phylogenetic, and subsampling analyses to assess the robustness of estimates for HIV's historical origin.
The study analyzes whole genomes and gene sequences to relate influenza A/H1N1pdm evolutionary diversity, antigenic mutations, introductions, and national migration hubs in China.
The title identifies a fatal severe-fever-with-thrombocytopenia infection accompanied by phylodynamic analysis in a newly affected Chinese province, providing an applied molecular-epidemiology lead.
The study uses molecular epidemiology, phylodynamics, reproduction-number estimation, and an updateable Nextstrain dataset to reconstruct an HAV outbreak among men who have sex with men.
The study applies separate Bayesian phylodynamic analyses to all eleven genome segments to infer the geographic and temporal ancestry of two rotavirus genotypes.
This multidisciplinary synthesis explains how epidemiology, virology, genetics, ecology, and evolutionary biology can jointly inform understanding and control of SARS-CoV-2.
The study applies phylodynamic and spatiotemporal analyses to thousands of SARS-CoV-2 genomes to characterize changing viral-type frequencies and a putative global selective sweep.
The study integrates phylogenetics, selection analysis, and recombination detection to characterize infectious bronchitis virus diversity and vaccine mismatch in Saudi poultry.
The paper uses simulations to diagnose biases from deduplication and subsampling and introduces PIQMEE for efficient Bayesian analysis of large pathogen datasets containing duplicate sequences.
The study integrates multiplex diagnostics, metagenomic sequencing, phylogenetics, and phylogeography to construct a national picture of respiratory-virus epidemiology in Vietnam.
This review explains how within-host viral diversity is sampled, measured, compared, and linked to experimental evolution and population-level phylodynamics.
This preprint reports the same Chicago SARS-CoV-2 genomes, three clades, and viral-load comparison as the subsequently published journal record PMID:33186810.
The study uses phylodynamic clustering and lineage-defining mutations to trace the international dissemination of early SARS-CoV-2 genomic groups.
The study combines phylogenetics, molecular clocks, coalescent models, and skyline plots to infer the timing and historical routes of HTLV-1 introductions into Iran.
The study integrates outbreak investigation, viral sequencing, phylogenetics, and phylodynamics to characterize the spread and long-term diversity of echovirus 30 in China.
The study develops a phylodynamic model calibrated with known transmission pairs to test how source-partner infection stage affects the number of transmitted HIV founder variants.
The paper introduces a coalescent reassortment-network model and an MCMC algorithm for jointly estimating reassortment histories and rates from segmented-virus genomes.
The study applies phylodynamic and biostatistical analyses to reconstruct the temporal, geographic, and cross-host spread of avian metapneumovirus across Europe.
The study combines phylodynamics, production-system movements, seasonality, and spatial environmental covariates to distinguish local and external PRRSV dissemination among pig farms.
The study applies phylogenetic model comparison and phylodynamics to estimate SARS-CoV-2 origin, evolutionary rate, and reproduction-number changes during Chinese control measures.
The study analyzes regional SARS-CoV-2 genomes and co-evolving mutations to characterize the geographic emergence and possible transmission routes of dominant variants in Southeast Asia.

The study combines animal infection experiments, diagnostic cross-reactivity assessment, assay development, and phylodynamics to characterize a newly identified ovine pestivirus and its cross-species ancestry.
The study uses Italian SARS-CoV-2 genomes and Bayesian phylodynamics to estimate introductions, epidemic timing, reproduction-number changes, and early cryptic circulation.
The study combines large-scale phylogenetics, Bayesian demographic reconstruction, selection analysis, and immune-escape mapping to compare growing HIV clusters in China.
The study integrates market surveillance, viral isolation, phylogenetics, and Bayesian host-geographic analysis to infer Newcastle disease virus sources and transmission hosts in China.
